1: The terumah (sacred portion) for the temple: Uvechalleqchem et-ha'aretz benachalah tarimu terumah lADONAI ('when you apportion the land as an inheritance, you shall offer a contribution to YHWH'). The new allocation begins with the sacred district at the land's center — the arrangement is theocentric: YHWH's domain at the center, then the priestly portion, then the Levitical portion, then the city, with the nasi's territory flanking both sides (Ezekiel 45:7). The terumah concept (lifted portion, elevated offering) from Exodus 25:2-3 becomes here a geographic category — a lifted-out zone of sacred territory.
2: The holy precinct dimensions: 500 by 500 square with 50 cubits of open space around it. The temple precinct (25,000 by 25,000 cubits holy area) contains within it a 500-cubit-square core for the sanctuary itself. The open space (miqreh) around the sanctuary functions as a buffer — keeping the most sacred structure physically separated from the surrounding territory. This spatial separation echoes the concentric holiness zones of the tabernacle: court, holy place, holy of holies — here translated into a geographic/urban planning principle.
3: The holy portion for the sanctuary: From the 25,000 cubit measure (east-west) the portion belonging to the sanctuary (qodesh qodashim) — the innermost district. The dimensions create nested zones of decreasing holiness radiating outward from the sanctuary: the innermost square → the priestly housing area → the Levitical territory → the city → the agricultural/nasi's land. This zoning plan makes visible what the temple's inner architecture already expressed: sacred reality diminishes in intensity as you move away from its source.
4: The priestly portion: The 25,000 by 10,000 cubit strip within the holy portion belongs to the priests who minister in the sanctuary. They will use it for houses and as a sacred place for the sanctuary (liqodesh lamiqdash). The priests' residential allocation is tied to the sanctuary — their housing exists within the sacred zone, reflecting the principle that priestly life is an extension of temple service. This contrasts with the monarchic period when priestly families could reside anywhere; the new order grounds them geographically at the sanctuary.
5: The Levitical portion: An equal 25,000 by 10,000 cubit strip for the Levites who serve in the house (mesharetey habayit). Twenty chambers (lishkot) as their possession. The Levites receive the same geographic extent as the priests but on the outer ring — their spatial position reflects their functional position: supporting the priestly service from a step removed. The twenty chambers (esrim lishkot) presumably house the Levitical families in communal clusters appropriate to their work-bands.
6: The city portion: 5,000 cubits wide and 25,000 cubits long, beside the holy terumah — this belongs to the whole house of Israel. The city territory is the broadest category: unlike the priestly and Levitical strips which belong to specific groups, the city's supporting hinterland belongs to "the whole house of Israel" (lechol beit Yisrael). This democratic allocation ensures that the city (which includes the temple) is the possession of the entire covenant community, not of a privileged class. The city's inclusivity counters the priestly-only exclusivity of the inner zones.
7: The nasi's (prince's) land: Velanasi mizzo uvemizo ('and to the prince, on either side'): the nasi's land flanks the sacred terumah and the city territory on both east and west sides, stretching to the eastern and western sea coasts. This allocation ensures the nasi is never landlocked or land-poor — he has territory on both sides of the central sacred district. The flanking position means the prince surrounds the sacred center without encroaching on it, representing his role as protector and patron of the sacred space without being its master.
8: The land as the prince's to prevent oppression: La'aretz yihyeh lo lesegulah be'Yisrael ('it shall be his land as a possession in Israel'). The explicit purpose: the princes shall no longer oppress my people, and the land shall be given to the house of Israel by their tribes. The Ezekiel vision emerged from the context of monarchic land-grabbing (1 Kgs 21 — Naboth's vineyard being the paradigmatic case; Micah 2:1-2; Isaiah 5:8). The nasi's guaranteed territorial portion eliminates the economic pressure that drove monarchic land seizures: he already has enough.
9: Direct address to the princes: "Enough!": Koh amar Adonai YHWH rav lakhem nesiey Yisrael ('thus says the Lord YHWH: enough, princes of Israel!'). The divine rebuke names two specific crimes: chamas umeshod ('violence and oppression/plunder'). Hasevu umishpat utzedeqah asu ('remove your violence and oppression and execute justice and righteousness'). The prophetic tradition's central social-justice demand is repeated here: justice and righteousness (mishpat utzedeqah) are the standing requirements for leadership. Isaiah 1:17; Amos 5:24; Jeremiah 22:3 — the same pair appears throughout the prophetic corpus as the non-negotiable standard for covenant leaders.
10: Standardized weights and measures: Mozney tzedeq ve'eifat tzedeq uvat tzedeq yihyeh lakhem ('just balances, just ephah, and just bath shall you have'). The demand for standardized weights and measures is a covenant-justice requirement rooted in Leviticus 19:35-36, Deuteronomy 25:13-15, and Proverbs 11:1, Ezekiel 16:11, Ezekiel 20:10, Ezekiel 20:23. Moznei tzedeq (just balances): the balance-scale was the primary commercial measurement tool in the ancient Near East; inaccurate scales were a systematic mechanism for economic exploitation, benefiting sellers who used underweight scales and buyers who used overweight scales depending on context.
11: Ephah and bath: unified capacity measures: Ha'eifah vehabet tochen echad yihyeh ('the ephah and the bath shall be the same capacity'). The ephah was the dry measure for grain; the bath was the liquid measure for oil and wine. Equating them establishes a unified system: both = 1/10 of a homer/kor. The archaeological record shows considerable variation in actual vessel capacities labeled "bath" — finds from excavations at Tell Beit Mirsim and Lachish show bath-jar fragments with wide size variation, confirming that the standardization called for here addressed a real commercial problem. A homer/kor of ten ephahs/baths creates a clean decimal system.
12: Shekel and maneh standards: Twenty gerahs = one shekel; sixty shekels = one maneh. The shekel standard (20 gerahs) matches Exodus 30:13; Leviticus 27:25; Numbers 3:47. The maneh at 60 shekels differs from some other ancient Near Eastern maneh standards (the Babylonian maneh = 60 shekels, consistent with Ezekiel's Babylonian context). The fact that Ezekiel specifies 60 shekels per maneh may reflect a correction of Judahite practice that had drifted from the Babylonian standard, or an alignment with the exilic community's commercial context in Babylon.
13: The contribution rates: Zot ha-terumah asher tarimu ('this is the contribution you shall offer'). 1/6 of an ephah from each homer of wheat; 1/6 of an ephah from each homer of barley. The contribution rates are modest fractions — not onerous — establishing a regular transfer from agricultural production to the sanctuary economy. The specific rate (1/60 for wheat: 0.1 ephah = 1/60 of a homer if homer=6 ephahs, or 1/6 if homer=1 ephah) places this within the tithe tradition while specifying which portion goes directly to the sacred terumah as opposed to other tithes.
14: Oil contribution: The oil contribution: 1/10 of a bath from each kor (since 10 baths = 1 kor/homer). The bath of oil gives 1/10 of a kor, making the proportion consistent. Oil (shemen) was one of the principal agricultural products of Canaan/Israel alongside grain and wine (Deuteronomy 7:13; Ezekiel 11:14; Hosea 2:8-9). Its inclusion in the contribution system reflects both its economic significance and its liturgical centrality — the lamp-oil, anointing oil, and cereal-offering oil were all critical to temple functioning.
15: Sheep contribution for offerings: One sheep from every two hundred from the watered pastures of Israel (mimashqeh Yisrael). This flock-contribution supports the grain offerings, burnt offerings, and peace offerings that effect atonement. Lechapper aleichem ('to make atonement for you'): the explicit purpose of the contribution system is atonement-maintenance. The entire economic structure of contributions, weights, and measures is designed not for institutional self-enrichment but for the continuous maintenance of the atonement mechanisms that sustain Israel's covenantal relationship with YHWH.
16: All the people's contribution: Kol-ha'am ha'aretz yihyu el-haterumah hazot ('all the people of the land shall be for this contribution'). The contribution system is not optional or merely aspirational — all the people of the land participate. The nasi is the manager of this collective contribution system, not merely a contributor himself. He receives (collects from the people) and provides (gives to the sanctuary) — the financial intermediary between the people and the temple economy. This establishes the nasi's role as the sanctuary's fiscal patron without making him its owner.
17: The nasi's liturgical provision: Ve'al-hanasi yihyeh haolot vehaminchah vehanasekh bamoadim uvechodashim uveshabbatot ('and upon the prince shall be the burnt offerings, grain offerings, and drink offerings, at feasts, new moons, and Sabbaths'). The nasi bears responsibility for the public worship cycle. This is the liturgical corollary of his territorial provision: just as his land allocation ensures he cannot be economically coercive (Ezekiel 45:8), his fiscal responsibility for the public cult ensures the cult is adequately funded. The three-tier calendar (feasts, new moons, Sabbaths) represents the full rhythm of Israel's worship year.
18: First month, first day: sanctuary purification: Barishon be'echad lachodesh tiqach par ben-baqar tamim vechateta et-hamiqdash ('in the first month, on the first day of the month, you shall take a bull without blemish and purify the sanctuary'). The yearly calendar of purification begins here: the New Year purification of the sanctuary on 1 Nisan (the spring New Year). This is analogous to but not identical with the Day of Atonement purification of Lev 16 (10 Tishri). Ezekiel's calendar separates the priestly purity calendar from the Levitical 7th-month calendar, using a first-month purification for the temple building.
19: Blood on the doorposts and altar: The priest takes some of the blood of the purification offering and applies it to the doorposts of the temple, the four corners of the ledge of the altar, and the doorposts of the inner court gate. The blood-smearing ritual echoes the Passover blood on doorposts (Exodus 12:7, 22-23) and the altar-consecration blood of Exodus 29:12 and Leviticus 8:15. In both cases, blood marks a threshold as YHWH's territory — the purification blood restores the sacred markers of ownership and consecration that may have become "dulled" through the previous year's ritual use.
20: The seventh day of the first month: The same purification is performed on the 7th day of the first month, for anyone who sinned inadvertently or through ignorance (bishgagah umin-peti). The inadvertent-sin category (shegagah) from Numbers 15:22-29 requires atonement for both individual and communal unintentional violations. The repetition of the sanctuary purification on the 7th day creates a week-long purification period that cleanses both the structure (1st day) and the accumulated inadvertent offenses of the community (7th day). The sanctuary is being reset to a pure starting state for the year.
21: Passover: 14th of the first month: Barishon bearba'ah asar yom lachodesh yihyeh lakhem hapesach chag shiv'at yamim matzot ye'achel ('in the first month, on the fourteenth day, you shall have the Passover, a feast of seven days, unleavened bread shall be eaten'). Passover remains the foundational commemorative feast — the exodus from Egypt. The seven days of unleavened bread correspond to Exodus 12:14-20; Leviticus 23:6-8; Numbers 28:17-25. The Passover occupies the same calendar position as in the Pentateuch but here is described as a gift of the nasi to the congregation — it is incorporated into the prince's liturgical responsibility (Ezekiel 45:17).
22: Passover sin offering: bull for the prince: On the Passover day, the nasi shall provide a bull as a sin offering for himself and all the people of the land (vela'am ha'aretz par chatat). The nasi's sin offering is the first sacrifice of the Passover — acknowledging that even the prince stands before YHWH in need of atonement. This leveling function of the sacrifice system is consistent throughout the Torah's sacrificial code: the anointed priest (Leviticus 4:3), the ruler (Leviticus 4:22), and the common person (Leviticus 4:27) all bring sin offerings, with the higher role bringing the more costly animal.
23: The seven-day Passover burnt offering: Seven bulls and seven rams daily for seven days (shiv'at yamim ya'aseh olah lADONAI shiv'at parim veshiv'at eilim temimim liom). This seven-fold offering structure mirrors the Numbers 28:19 Passover offering schedule. The sin offering is one male goat daily. The seven-day intensity of burnt offerings during Passover reflects the magnitude of the exodus-event being commemorated: the nation's founding liberation from Egypt. The sevenfold repetition (7 bulls + 7 rams × 7 days) creates an arithmetic expression of completeness-within-completeness.
24: Grain and oil offerings accompanying the Passover: An ephah of grain offering per bull and ram, and a hin of oil per ephah. The accompaniment of grain and oil with burnt offerings follows the standard formula of Numbers 15:1-12 where each animal offering is attended by specific grain and liquid measure proportions. The hin of oil per ephah of grain is a generous measure — approximately 1:1 by volume — ensuring substantial quantities of oil are consecrated in the grain offerings. These proportions ground the temple economy in the agricultural reality of grain and olive cultivation in the land.
25: Feast of Tabernacles: 7th month, 15th day: Bashvi'i bachamisha asar yom lachodesh bachag ya'aseh ka'elleh shiv'at yamim ('in the seventh month, on the fifteenth day of the month, at the feast, shall he do like these things for seven days'). Sukkot (Tabernacles/Booths) receives the same seven-day burnt offering and sin offering schedule as Passover — a symmetry that brackets the agricultural year: Passover (1 Nisan, spring barley/grain beginning) and Tabernacles (15 Tishri, fall harvest completion). The two feasts frame the agricultural year with its bookend sacrifices, sanctifying the entire agricultural cycle within the covenant calendar.
